raw_text: I saw HIM near the DOCKS on Tuesday!!!   He was with a man called Reeves-or so I thought.  [REDACTED] told me to keep quiet.  CASE-42 is none of my business.
The docks were EMPTY that night.  I heard footsteps on the cobbles.  Someone shouted Reeves' name.  Then silence.

Margaret Hayes is mistaken.  I was at the warehouse until midnight.  The dock workers had already gone home.  Reeves was not there-I am certain of it.  Ask Whitfield; he was drinking at the Anchor.  CASE-42 has everyone on edge.
The accountant left the office at six.  He carried a LEATHER satchel.  [REDACTED] followed him down River Lane.
I saw the numbers guy near the station.  CASE-17 again.  He looked nervous.
Nothing unusual at the warehouse that week.
A black motorcar stopped outside my shop.  Two men got out.  One wore a grey overcoat.  [REDACTED] stayed in the car.
CASE-88 is a mess.  I told the chief already.  The grey overcoat man came back on Thursday.  He asked for directions to the docks.

I have worked the docks for twenty years.  On the night in question I was mending nets by lamp-light.  I saw two figures near pier seven.  One was tall and broad-could have been Reeves, though I cannot swear to it.  The other was shorter, wearing a cap.  They argued.  I heard the word warehouse.  Then a door slammed on the quay.  I stayed quiet.  Men like that do not like witnesses.  Margaret Hayes may have seen the same pair from the far end of the jetty.  Whitfield was drunk; I would not trust his account.  Marsh claims she was elsewhere, but the warehouse clock was stopped that week.  CASE-42 has made everyone nervous.  Ships still come in.  The fog was thick.  I could not make out faces after they turned away.
The motorcar had no plates.  Grey overcoat again.
